metadata.title: Multiple Endpoints in Clinical Trials: Guidance for Industry

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must prospectively specify all endpoints in the primary and secondary families and all data analyses that will be performed to test hypotheses about those endpoints to control the Type I error rate.
rules.verbatim_quote: To control the Type I error rate, it is critical that sponsors prospectively specify the following: all endpoints in the primary and secondary families (see section III. for definitions). all data analyses that will be performed to test hypotheses about the prespecified endpoints, regardless of whether they are considered primary or secondary.

rules.rule_text: The statistical analysis plan must describe a testing procedure for multiple hypotheses with proper control of the overall Type I error rate.
rules.verbatim_quote: For a study with multiple endpoints, the analysis plan should describe the testing procedure for the hypotheses being tested with a proper control of overall Type I error rate.

rules.rule_text: The statistical analysis plan must not be changed after unmasking of treatment assignments and performing statistical analyses.
rules.verbatim_quote: The statistical analysis plan should not be changed after unmasking of treatment assignments and performing statistical analyses.
rules.exceptions: Changes prior to data analysis are permitted if appropriate multiplicity adjustments are performed.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must control the overall Type I error probability to maintain the rate at 2.5% or below for one-sided tests when evaluating multiple endpoints.
rules.verbatim_quote: There are various approaches that can be planned prospectively and applied to maintain the overall Type I error rate at 2.5% or below.

rules.rule_text: Descriptive analyses not included in the prespecified multiple-testing strategy must not be presented in FDA-approved labeling in ways that imply statistically rigorous conclusions or convey certainty about effects not supported by that trial.
rules.verbatim_quote: A descriptive analysis that is not included in the prespecified multiple-testing strategy should not be presented in FDA-approved labeling in ways that imply a statistically rigorous conclusion or convey certainty about the effects that are not supported by that trial.
rules.exceptions: Descriptive analyses can be included in FDA-approved labeling without presenting p-values.

rules.rule_text: The primary endpoint family must be designated to establish the effect(s) of the drug and will be the basis for concluding that the study meets its objective; the overall Type I error rate must be controlled for both primary and secondary endpoint families together.
rules.source_section: Section III.A.3, Selecting and Interpreting the Endpoints in the Primary and Secondary Endpoint Families
rules.verbatim_quote: Positive results on the secondary endpoints can be interpretable if there is first a demonstration of a treatment effect on the primary endpoint family (O'Neill 1997). The overall Type I error rate should control for the primary and secondary endpoint families all together.

rules.rule_text: Secondary endpoints that can potentially provide evidence of additional effects of the drug on the disease or condition must be included in the Type I error control plan.
rules.source_section: Section III.A.2, Secondary and Exploratory Endpoint Families
rules.verbatim_quote: As a general principle, it is important to include the secondary endpoints that can potentially provide evidence of additional effects of the drug on the disease or condition in the Type I error control plan.
rules.exceptions: Exploratory endpoints do not need multiplicity adjustment because they are generally not used to support conclusions.

rules.rule_text: When co-primary endpoints are used (each must be demonstrated to conclude efficacy), increasing alpha for individual co-primary endpoints above the targeted alpha is not acceptable.
rules.verbatim_quote: Increasing α for each co-primary endpoint is not acceptable because doing so may undermine the ability to interpret a treatment effect on each disease aspect considered critical to show that the drug is effective in support of approval.

rules.rule_text: When multiple primary endpoints are used and any one success is sufficient for effectiveness, the Type I error rate for the primary endpoint family must be controlled.
rules.source_section: Section III.C.2, When Demonstration of a Treatment Effect on at Least One of Several Primary Endpoints Is Sufficient
rules.verbatim_quote: This use of multiple endpoints creates a multiplicity problem because there are several ways for the study to successfully demonstrate a treatment effect. Control of the Type I error rate for the primary endpoint family is critical.

rules.rule_text: The choice of components for a composite endpoint must be made carefully; the composite should include components with reasonably similar clinical importance.
rules.verbatim_quote: The choice of the components of a composite endpoint should be made carefully. The treatment effect on the composite event rate can be interpreted as characterizing the overall clinical effect when the individual events all have reasonably similar clinical importance.

rules.rule_text: Results for each component event of a composite endpoint must be individually examined and included in study reports.
rules.source_section: Section III.D.1, Evaluating and Reporting the Results of Composite Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: Results for each component event should therefore be individually examined and should be included in study reports. These analyses will not alter a conclusion about the statistical significance of the composite primary endpoint.

rules.rule_text: If individual component endpoints of a composite are to be tested as distinct hypotheses, those hypotheses must be part of the prospectively specified statistical analysis plan with multiplicity accounting.
rules.source_section: Section III.D.1, Evaluating and Reporting the Results of Composite Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: If there is an interest in analyzing one or more of the components of a composite endpoint as distinct hypotheses to demonstrate effects of the drug, the hypotheses should be part of the prospectively specified statistical analysis plan that accounts for the multiplicity this analysis will entail.

rules.rule_text: Pre-specification of specific components or subdomains of a multi-component endpoint as endpoints with appropriate multiplicity control is required if the intent is to demonstrate a drug effect on those components in addition to the overall multi-component endpoint.
rules.source_section: Section III.D.2, Evaluating and Reporting the Results on Other Multi-Component Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: Pre-specification of specific components or subdomains as endpoints with appropriate multiplicity control is recommended if the intent is to demonstrate an effect of a drug on one or more of these endpoints in addition to the overall multi-component endpoint.

rules.rule_text: The overall Type I error rate for a family of endpoints is typically held to 0.05 for two-sided or 0.025 for one-sided tests; statistical methods controlling this rate are required to permit an effectiveness conclusion on individual endpoints.
rules.verbatim_quote: This error rate is typically held to 0.05 (or 0.025 for one-sided tests). Statistical methods that control this error rate at the desired level can permit an effectiveness conclusion on individual endpoints.

rules.rule_text: When non-inferiority and superiority are tested for a single primary endpoint plus at least one additional endpoint, multiplicity issues arise and adjustments must be made to control the overall Type I error probability.
rules.verbatim_quote: However, if at least one more endpoint is included for testing, then multiplicity issues arise, and adjustments should be made to control the overall Type I error probability.
rules.exceptions: Non-inferiority and superiority on the same single endpoint can be tested without multiplicity adjustment because the null hypotheses are naturally ordered.

rules.rule_text: Sample size calculations for studies with co-primary endpoints must account for the increased Type II error rate; individual endpoints should typically be powered at approximately 90% to ensure overall study power of at least 80%.
rules.source_section: Section III.B, Type II Error Rate and Sample Size
rules.verbatim_quote: To maintain desired study power, a larger sample size is recommended, and the individual endpoints could be powered at approximately 90% to ensure the probability of success is at least 80%.
rules.exceptions: Calculation differs if endpoints are highly positively correlated or if power was not equal for each endpoint.

rules.rule_text: Sample size calculations must account for the statistical adjustments needed to control the Type I error rate for multiplicity.
rules.source_section: Section III.B, Type II Error Rate and Sample Size
rules.verbatim_quote: The sample size calculation may need to account for the statistical adjustments to control the Type I error rate for multiplicity. For example, if a lower α is used for a study endpoint, then the sample size should be adjusted to provide desired statistical power for this endpoint.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors should discuss early with FDA any plans to use alternative statistical frameworks (e.g., Bayesian approaches) instead of the hypothesis-testing framework for specific development programs.
rules.source_section: Section II.A, Demonstrating the Study Objective of Effectiveness
rules.verbatim_quote: This guidance focuses on a statistical framework based on hypothesis testing. Sponsors should discuss early with FDA plans to use other approaches (e.g., Bayesian approaches) for a specific development program such as for pediatrics.

rules.rule_text: Clinically critical endpoints that are too infrequent for use as a primary endpoint must still be collected, analyzed, and can be included as a secondary endpoint in a composite primary endpoint trial.
rules.source_section: Section III.C.5, Clinically Critical Endpoints Too Infrequent for Use as a Primary Endpoint
rules.verbatim_quote: Even if relatively few of these events are expected to occur in the trial, they can be included in a composite endpoint (see section III.C.3.) and also designated as a planned secondary endpoint to potentially support a conclusion regarding effect on that separate endpoint, if the effect of the drug on the composite primary endpoint is demonstrated.

rules.rule_text: The examination of all components of a composite endpoint is always necessary, but whether multiplicity adjustment should be made depends on the purpose of the analysis.
rules.verbatim_quote: The examination of the components is always necessary, but whether multiplicity adjustment should be made depends on the purpose. If the intent is to better understand the demonstrated effect on the composite, then no adjustment is recommended. ... If the intent is to establish additional effects of the drug, then multiplicity adjustment should be made.

rules.rule_text: The Bonferroni method must have prespecified weights for endpoints that sum to 1.0; weights must be prespecified in the design of the trial.
rules.verbatim_quote: The Bonferroni test can also be performed with different weights assigned to endpoints, with the sum of the relative weights equal to 1.0 (e.g., 0.4, 0.3, 0.2, and 0.1 for four endpoints). These weights should be prespecified in the design of the trial, taking into consideration the clinical importance of the endpoints, the likelihood of success, or other factors.

rules.rule_text: When using the Fixed-Sequence Method, the sequence of testing must be prospectively specified and once a failure occurs in the sequence, no further testing is permissible.
rules.verbatim_quote: Such a testing procedure requires (1) prospective specification of the testing sequence and (2) no further testing once the sequence breaks; that is, further testing stops as soon as there is a failure of an endpoint in the sequence to show significance at level alpha (e.g., α = 0.05).

rules.rule_text: In the graphical approach, the sum of all endpoint-specific alpha levels must equal the total alpha level available for the study (the overall Type I error rate).
rules.source_section: Appendix, Section 8, Graphical Approaches Based on Sequentially Rejective Tests
rules.verbatim_quote: A key requirement is that the sum of all of the endpoint-specific alpha levels is equal to the total alpha level available for the study (the overall Type I error rate). At each step of the algorithm, endpoints are tested at the endpoint-specific significance levels using Bonferroni procedure.

rules.rule_text: Use of the Hochberg procedure requires that the assumptions of the procedure are met; it is not assumption-free and may fail to control the overall Type I error rate for negatively correlated tests.
rules.verbatim_quote: The Hochberg procedure is known to provide adequate overall alpha-control for independent endpoint tests or for positively correlated dependent tests with standard test statistics in some cases (e.g., the test statistics are jointly bivariate normal). ... Various simulation experiments for the general case (e.g., for more than two endpoints with unequal correlation structures) indicate that the Hochberg procedure usually will, but is not guaranteed to, control the overall Type I error rate for positively correlated endpoints, but fails to do so for some negatively correlated tests.
rules.exceptions: Bonferroni and Holm procedures are assumption-free and can be applied without concern for endpoint types or correlation structure.

rules.rule_text: For gatekeeping testing strategies with serial gatekeeping, if at least one null hypothesis in the primary family fails to be rejected, the primary family criterion has not been met and the secondary endpoint family must not be tested.
rules.verbatim_quote: If, however, at least one of the null hypotheses of the primary family fails to be rejected, the primary family criterion has not been met and the secondary endpoint family is not tested.
rules.exceptions: A parallel gatekeeping strategy allows secondary endpoint family testing when at least one endpoint in the first family has shown statistical significance.

rules.rule_text: The multiplicity method must be decided prospectively and sponsors should select the most powerful method that is suitable for the design and objective of the study while maintaining Type I error rate control.
rules.verbatim_quote: The choice of the method to use for a specific clinical trial will depend on the objectives and the design of the trial, as well as the knowledge of the drug being developed and the clinical setting. The method, however, should be decided upon prospectively. ... Sponsors should consider the variety of methods available and in the prospective analysis plan select the most powerful method that is suitable for the design and objective of the study and maintains Type I error rate control.

rules.rule_text: For hypothesis tests based on clinical trial outcomes to support drug approval, both the null and alternative hypotheses must be specified for each chosen endpoint in advance of conducting a clinical trial.
rules.source_section: Section II.A, Demonstrating the Study Objective of Effectiveness
rules.verbatim_quote: This approach begins with stating the relevant hypotheses for a chosen endpoint. In the simplest situation where the aim is to demonstrate the superiority of a test drug over control, two mutually exclusive hypotheses are specified for the endpoint in advance of conducting a clinical trial.

rules.rule_text: To establish clinical benefit for drugs treating diseases with two or more critically important features, co-primary endpoints require demonstration of treatment effect on all of those features.
rules.verbatim_quote: Multiple primary endpoints become co-primary endpoints when demonstrating an effect on each of the endpoints is critical to concluding that a drug is effective.

rules.rule_text: The use of more than two co-primary endpoints must be carefully considered because of the resulting loss of statistical power.
rules.verbatim_quote: In general, unless clinically very important, the use of more than two co-primary endpoints should be carefully considered because of the loss of power.
rules.exceptions: Co-primary endpoints in combination vaccine trials where demonstration is required per pathogen are acceptable.

rules.rule_text: The overall Type I error rate for conclusions about drug effects on one-sided tests must be maintained at no more than 2.5% (1 chance in 40) to avoid false positive conclusions about effectiveness.
rules.verbatim_quote: The most widely used values for α are 0.05 for two-sided tests and 0.025 for one-sided tests. In the case of one-sided tests, an α of 0.025 means that the probability of falsely concluding a beneficial effect of the drug when none exists is no more than 2.5%, or 1 chance in 40.

rules.rule_text: Analyses of individual component endpoints of a composite or multi-component endpoint must be individually considered pre-specified if the intent is to draw definitive conclusions about specific component effects.
rules.source_section: Section III.D.2, Evaluating and Reporting the Results on Other Multi-Component Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: Analysis of the study results on the individual components is usually important but, as stated previously, if undertaken, should not be presented in FDA-approved labeling in ways that imply a statistically rigorous conclusion or convey certainty about the effects that are not supported by that trial.

rules.rule_text: Resampling-based multiple-testing procedures require large study sample sizes, particularly bootstrap methods, and often require simulations to ensure adequate Type I error rate control.
rules.verbatim_quote: These methods, consequently, usually require large study sample sizes (particularly bootstrap methods) and often require simulations to ensure the data-based distribution of the test statistics from the limited trial data is applicable and to ensure adequate Type I error rate control.

rules.rule_text: Failure to appropriately control the Type I error rate increases the risk of a false positive conclusion about effectiveness, which can lead to false or misleading representations regarding a drug's effects.
rules.verbatim_quote: Basing a conclusion on an analysis where the risk of false conclusions has not been appropriately controlled can lead to false or misleading representations regarding a drug's effects.

rules.rule_text: Endpoints in adequate and well-controlled drug trials must be grouped hierarchically according to clinical importance and expected drug effects, with critical endpoints for approval designated as primary.
rules.source_section: Section III.A, The Hierarchy of Families of Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: Endpoints in adequate and well-controlled drug trials are usually grouped hierarchically, often according to their clinical importance, but also taking into consideration the expected frequency of the endpoint events and anticipated drug effects. The critical determination for grouping endpoints is whether they are intended to establish effectiveness to support approval or intended to demonstrate additional meaningful effects. Endpoints critical to establish effectiveness for approval are often designated as primary endpoints.

rules.rule_text: When the null hypothesis for a vaccine trial minimum effect is modified to incorporate the smallest clinically meaningful effect, formal statistical testing criteria must be adjusted accordingly.
rules.source_section: Section II.A, Demonstrating the Study Objective of Effectiveness
rules.verbatim_quote: Sometimes (e.g., in some vaccine trials), demonstration of an effect of at least some minimum size is considered critical for approval of a drug. In this case, if formal statistical testing is used for the demonstration, the null hypothesis might be modified to incorporate the smallest clinically meaningful effect that could be accepted.
